text: more or less the universe of questions. You'll get almost like the same flavor of questions, but maybe framed slightly differently or phrased slightly differently from other investors. And your ability to just like create a central repository of all your due diligence materials and then actually just have AI write the first responses is kind of remarkable. So we're pretty acquisitive. We do a bunch of corp dev and it's not like they're like particularly sophisticated data rooms at our scale where we require companies, but it's usually like an investor presentation, some kind of Excel sheet with some stuff, some employee data, and we built little bots that would basically just review data room and give us the output on one page that we then look at when we discuss the deal in our weekly M&A meeting. So it's just stuff like that. You can, as a consequence, have time to look at more deals simultaneously and make it really easy for people that sit on the M&A committee to be like, oh, we should look at this. I read the one-pager. Not interesting. Let's move on. So kind of another use case of how to be more efficient. Sure. Very cool. I do want to kind of give an early plug. In a couple minutes, we're going to open up to audience questions. So kind of think, and we'll definitely circle back. And back to you, Martin. You know, we're all kind of private companies, you know, hopefully for now, up here. Can you build kind of ASC-606 app like other? As you think about kind of like SOX compliance and going to your audit committee meeting, and like you did this, you did what with your contract? and then how do you prove and kind of represent, like how are you building comfort around controls and audit? It's definitely a tough topic. We're actually working directly with Deloitte to make sure that they are okay with like, you know, we still, while we make life easier for ourselves, we still do manual review of these contracts, right? But we're getting feedback from auditors. So like what is an appropriate use of AI in this process and what is not. So hopefully that the field of auditing will also evolve in the AI age. But candidly, we're not quite there yet, right? So there's still manual reviews involved. We just use the tools to make life easier for ourselves, not to completely automate the workflow. Yeah, not that I'm trying to make a plug for Deloitte necessarily, but I think having an upfront conversation with your auditors to understand their appetite and their comfort level around your use of the tools, I think, was actually one of the reasons we went with Deloitte as our auditor because we felt like they were at least through how they articulated to us and we experienced this going through an audit with them just a little bit more on the front foot about thinking about okay we are going to be using AI to help improve workflows and our finance work, making sure that that wasn't just a non-starter for them, that our audit work would almost be the thing that would hamstring our ability to move forward from a workflow perspective. They want to say yes. They do. Yeah. Yeah. I think that it's just it's it's hard because I mean look one thing that's actually good from an AI perspective is like if you think about it finance like there's a ton of rich data that underpin financial like information and it's also relatively verifiable like you know if it's correct or not and so in theory AI should actually do really well in finance because you can tell it you were right you were wrong and it learns so like there is a huge potential for AI from a finance perspective in my mind but it's just getting people comfortable that there's maybe a little less visibility or a little less manual sort of, you know, you can go and say to person X, hey, you messed up or person Y, you messed up because it's, you know, you can't bar an agent, so to speak. It is interesting because quality assurance is obviously an important part of a normal finance team, right? Some junior analyst did some work and, you know, like a senior manager will look at it and say, like, that was wrong or should we do it? But somehow if it's AI that does it, we trust it less than a kid straight out of college with no work experience. So I do think the world will evolve here as well. And the burden will be now on people actually reviewing the information, right? You know, what tests have you done to ensure that what came out of the underlying foundation models is actually accurate and representative? And it might end up being similar to something what the auditors do, right, when they do sampling. So I think you might be expected to do your own sampling based on, like, what the audit, what the LLMs fit out. Right now. So, yeah, basically probably more augmentation, not replacing kind of humans still in the loop. So any kind of brave souls out there? Any questions, please stand up in the front. Hi, I'm Andrew. We're one of Rex's customers. Love it. Ben didn't pay me to say that, but OK. I would love to see what is this partnership of a CFO role and a chief people officer role look like? where the push and pulls, and then with the mix of AI in there, how do you see this relationship evolving? And I'm on the other side of the GPU, so I'm curious to know from your perspective, how do you see this partnership at Workplace So I will be the first to say that I think the CFO relationship is one of the most important relationships that you can have as an organization because I think that you need to make sure that you both on the same page and you kind of have each other backs as it relates to the organizational change you trying to drive I think that there's always going to be a sort of natural, almost tension that exists between those organizations because there's a bit of an element of, you know, if you just be overly redundant, like finance people care about money and how much we're spending on certain things. And people officers can also care about, like, what's the employee experience, how are we setting up our employees for success. That obviously involves investments in learning development and tools and access and systems that we set up for success. I think what I would say with Archie Peeble, officer, who's phenomenal, a woman by the name of Heather Dunn, is just really making sure that we have continued conversations about like, what are our objectives? What are we trying to optimize for? We have weekly one on ones. We're like going through our teardown of like, here are all the big problems that we're trying to solve and work on them together. because one of the things that I will say is that it is challenging for an employee base, especially as a breakfast, like a 1,200-person company, to go through this sort of quasi-transformation because you have a lot of people you've hired that maybe weren't as AI-fluent, and now they're going through this organizational shift, and how do you make sure you can set them up for success? There's also the lingering question in the back of a lot of people's minds about, does this mean role eliminations? Does this mean reductions in headcount? I think the way we've articulated it, and Brex is fortunate that we're growing so fast so that we are able to have this luxury, is we're saying, hey, we don't anticipate this will lead to role reductions. It just means we'll be able to go further with the people we have on our staff today. And that's been a really helpful message that we've been able to articulate because, again, of the luxury and the position we're in. That may not necessarily be the case for all organizations, and I really respect that. And I think trying to have that sort of close partnership is really important so that you can be humane in all of this. like everyone is going through this change and I think making sure that a finance person isn't coming in like myself and saying oh we want to see you know 10% reduction in G&A arbitrarily because of AI operational improvements like actually working through with the people person to determine like how feasible is is that on what time horizon is that even like you know is that even where our G&A costs are like I mean we're now a sort of an office first company I can't make an office space agentic I mean maybe theoretically could in terms of access controls but like we still occupy certain space. So like making sure you have a really good grasp and relationship around what are the true drivers of these costs and how to move them forward Yeah we also work very closely our head of people and myself and we have way more problems than we have people to support said problems So we look at everything like what is like the most urgent needs and how do we staff towards them and what are areas where we can still make progress on things we like to work on and things we like to fix and are there areas where we can, rather than hire three more people into this area, we can use AI to support them, have them be more efficient, and push the envelope a little bit more around efficient teams. But yeah, it's not like we can say, because of AI we're not going to make drastic cuts in the business, but I do think you can make efficiency improvements. So we spent a lot of time and energy on that and making sure that we resource and staff the business correctly. That's great. Anyone else? I see someone. Tushar. Yeah, me. I think all three of you are fairly well-funded companies. So when you think about AI, and I think, Rex, you've gone through many transformations, so we'd love to hear your thoughts on this as well. But how do you think about efficiency calculations or the ROI on each of these, whether internal or external tools that you want to implement and have your teams use? Is it a dollar number? Is it time savings? Yeah, I mean, I think that is definitely a hard dynamic. And I think even before AI, when you would try to assess productivity, it was always a hard question for a CFO when someone was saying, hey, I'll get this new software and it will make us more productive. And you're like, okay, well, how are you quantifying that? Like, will it actually? And I think there's always been a little bit of awareness on the finance side about productivity gain assumptions when making the decision to leverage a new software or a new tool. I do think there is something different about AI in general as it relates to it is today, to Martin's point, really helping replace a lot of the more deterministic and more rote work that you can naturally say, okay, for example, I can easily say that our Sierra chatbot instance is saving us 15,000 hours worth of customer experience time over the course of the year by virtue of the chat containment rate that we're seeing, the speed to resolution for our customers, and then doing the relatively simple math to understand, OK, this saves us X number of customer agents that we otherwise don't need to hire into as the company scales. I think that becomes a little bit harder to determine when it comes to more on the engineering
